items.creator: CARE
items.title: Needs Assessment Working Group Bangladesh: Flash Flood & Landslides - Bangladesh | Flash Update #2, 11 July 2026)
items.author: CARE
items.content:
Country: Bangladesh
Source: CARE

Please refer to the attached file.

The situation continues to evolve, with forecasts indicating an additional 100–300 mm of rainfall through 12 July, while river levels remain elevated and flooding persists in several areas. The latest information compiled from government authorities, humanitarian partners, and field reports indicates that 10 districts have been affected, more than 1.07 million people have been impacted, over 36,000 people remain temporarily displaced, and 26 casualties have been reported. Infrastructure damage, access constraints, and disruption to basic services continue to affect affected communities.

The update also provides:

  • Latest impact overview and affected population estimates;
  • Humanitarian needs and priority concerns;
  • Government response and relief distributions;
  • Access and infrastructure disruptions;
  • Preliminary sectoral information from partner reports and assessments.

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Lebanon: Flash Update #42 - Escalation of hostilities in Lebanon (as of 9 July 2026)
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Lebanon
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

HIGHLIGHTS

  • At least 4,321 deaths and 12,204 injuries due to hostilities recorded by the Ministry of Public Health since 2 March.
  • 732,594 people having returned to their areas of origin, while 430,000 people remain displaced, including 33,343 Internally Displaced Persons (over 53 per cent women and girls) in 325 collective shelters as of 9 July.
  • Humanitarian needs remain acute in both displacement and return areas. Damage to homes, health facilities, water systems, electricity networks, and other critical infrastructure continues to affect access to essential services, while funding gaps and operational constraints are limiting the scale of the response. As of 9 July, the 2026 Lebanon Flash Appeal was only 42 per cent funded.

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Earthquakes in Venezuela: Situation Report #17 (10 July 2026, Time: 09:00 pm)
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

Situation Report No. 17
Earthquakes in Venezuela
10 July 2026
Time: 9:00 pm

Highlights

  • Following the 24 June earthquakes of magnitude 7.2 and 7.5 (18:00 local time), authorities report more than 1,100 aftershocks.
  • Official toll: 4,118 people dead and 16,740 injured.
  • A total of 6,462 people have been rescued.
  • Tom Fletcher, the United Nation’s humanitarian chief, concluded his four-day visit to the country and called for sustained international support for the Humanitarian Response Plan (HRP).
  • An additional appeal for US$298 million was launched to scale up the earthquake response and address the multisectoral needs of 1.3 million people over the next six months.
  • The United Nations system and its partner organizations, in coordination with the Government, are responding with multisectoral actions and will continue to scale up assistance to affected people.

Situation overview

Venezuela is facing a national emergency following two consecutive high-magnitude earthquakes (7.2 and 7.5) that struck on 24 June (18:00 local time) in the north-central region, with the epicentre located along the San Felipe–Yumare–Montalbán axis (Yaracuy/Carabobo). Authorities report 1,171 aftershocks.

Authorities report a toll of 4,118 people dead, 16,740 injured, and 6,462 rescued since the onset of the emergency. Authorities also reported that approximately 17,907 people lost their homes.

The Government response remains focused on search and rescue operations, the preparation and expansion of transitional camps and assistance to affected populations. The United Nations and its partner organizations, in coordination with the authorities, are implementing multisectoral response activities and will continue to scale up assistance to affected people. The United Nations Under-Secretary-General for Humanitarian Affairs and Emergency Relief Coordinator, Tom Fletcher, concluded his four-day visit to Venezuela on Friday and called on the international community to maintain its support for the Humanitarian Response Plan (HRP) to help address the most urgent needs of people in vulnerable situations.

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Terremotos en Venezuela: Reporte de situación #17 (10 de julio de 2026, Hora: 09:00 pm)
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

Reporte de situación N.º 17
Terremotos en Venezuela
10 de julio de 2026
Hora: 9:00 pm

Destacados

  • Tras los sismos de magnitud 7,2 y 7,5 del 24 de junio (18:00 hora local), las autoridades reportan más de 1.100 réplicas.
  • Balance oficial: 4.118 personas fallecidas y 16.740 heridas.
  • 6.462 personas han sido rescatadas.
  • Tom Fletcher, jefe humanitario de las Naciones Unidas, concluyó su visita de cuatro días al país e hizo un llamado a mantener el
    compromiso internacional con el Plan de Respuesta Humanitaria.
  • Se lanzó un llamamiento adicional por US$298 millones para ampliar la respuesta a los terremotos y atender las necesidades de 1,3 millones de personas durante los próximos seis meses.
  • El Sistema de Naciones Unidas y sus organizaciones socias, en
    coordinación con el gobierno, responden con acciones multisectoriales y continuarán ampliando la asistencia a las personas afectadas.

Situación general

Venezuela enfrenta una emergencia nacional tras dos sismos consecutivos de gran magnitud (7,2 y 7,5) ocurridos el 24 de junio (18:00, hora local) en la región centro-norte, con epicentro sobre el eje San Felipe - Yumare - Montalbán (Yaracuy/Carabobo). Las autoridades reportan 1.171 réplicas.

Las autoridades reportan un total de 4.118 personas fallecidas, 16.740 personas heridas y 6.462 personas rescatadas desde el inicio de la emergencia. Asimismo, indicaron que unas 17.907 personas quedaron sin vivienda.

La respuesta gubernamental mantiene el foco en las labores de búsqueda, la gestión de los campamentos transitorios y la atención de las personas afectadas. Naciones Unidas y sus organizaciones socias, en articulación con las autoridades, realizan acciones multisectoriales para las personas afectadas y continuarán ampliando la respuesta. El Subsecretario General de las Naciones Unidas para Asuntos Humanitarios y Coordinador del Socorro de Emergencia, Tom Fletcher, concluyó este viernes su visita de cuatro días a Venezuela e instó a la comunidad internacional a mantener su apoyo al Plan de Respuesta Humanitaria (PRH) para contribuir a la atención de las necesidades más urgentes de las personas en situación de mayor vulnerabilidad.

items.creator: International Organization for Migration
items.title: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of): Earthquake Response Situation Report #10 (9 July 2026)
items.author: International Organization for Migration
items.content:
Country: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Source: International Organization for Migration

Please refer to the attached file.

The tenth Situation Report provides an update on IOM’s response to the 24 June earthquakes in the Bolivarian Republic of Venezuela as of 9 July. It also includes the priorities of IOM’s Funding Appeal to scale up urgent assistance.

Highlights

In La Guaira, IOM and its partners strengthened assistance in transitory camps. At César Nieves Stadium, they continued improving spaces and distributed tarpaulins and kitchen sets, while at José María Vargas Sports Centre, services were expanded to include dental and psychiatric care, reaching 290 health and mental health consultations.

To date, IOM has reached 4,526 unique individuals through 7,273 assistance and distribution services, mainly in health, shelter and site coordination, and protection.

IOM maintains its USD 98 million Funding Appeal to assist 850,000 people and support 140 entities between July and December 2026.

IOM Activities

IOM continues supporting prioritized transitory camps in La Guaira, including César Nieves Stadium, José María Vargas Sports Centre and Playa Grande, through site coordination, shelter, registration, WASH, health and mental health, protection and information management.

At César Nieves Stadium, 341 tarpaulins and 334 kitchen sets were distributed. To date, 830 tarpaulins and 825 kitchen sets have been delivered.

At José María Vargas Sports Centre, a team of 12 professionals provided 290 health and mental health consultations, including dental care, internal medicine, paediatrics, gynaecology and obstetrics, psychiatry and psychology.

Registration continued at César Nieves Stadium, covering 489 families and 1,479 individuals, and at Playa Grande, covering 54 families and 160 individuals. Progress was also made on the Rapid Needs Assessment and on information management support to the Infrastructure and Temporary Accommodation Working Group.

IOM Planned Response

IOM will continue focusing on the improvement and management of transitory camps, WASH, health and MHPSS, protection, registration, needs assessments, mobility monitoring and inter-agency coordination.

Additional funding would enable IOM to scale up shelter assistance, essential items, early recovery, protection, health and mental health, data, DTM and coordination support.

items.creator: International Organization for Migration
items.title: Venezuela (República Bolivariana de): Respuesta a Terremotos - Reporte de Situación #10 (9 de julio de 2026)
items.author: International Organization for Migration
items.content:
Country: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Source: International Organization for Migration

Please refer to the attached file.

El décimo Reporte de Situación presenta una actualización sobre la respuesta de la OIM a los terremotos del 24 de junio en la República Bolivariana de Venezuela al 9 de julio. También incluye las prioridades del Llamamiento de Fondos de la OIM para ampliar la asistencia urgente.

Aspectos destacados

En La Guaira, la OIM y sus socios reforzaron la asistencia en campamentos transitorios. En el Estadio César Nieves continuaron adecuando espacios y distribuyeron lonas y sets de cocina, mientras que en el Polideportivo José María Vargas ampliaron la atención con servicios de odontología y psiquiatría, alcanzando 290 consultas de salud y salud mental.

A la fecha, la OIM ha alcanzado a 4.526 personas únicas mediante 7.273 asistencias y servicios de distribución, principalmente en salud, alojamiento y coordinación de sitios, y protección.

La OIM mantiene su Llamamiento de Fondos por USD 98 millones para asistir a 850.000 personas y apoyar a 140 entidades entre julio y diciembre de 2026.

Actividades de la OIM

La OIM continúa apoyando campamentos transitorios priorizados en La Guaira, incluidos el Estadio César Nieves, el Polideportivo José María Vargas y Playa Grande, mediante coordinación de sitios, alojamiento, registro, WASH, salud y salud mental, protección y gestión de información.

En el Estadio César Nieves, se distribuyeron 341 lonas plásticas y 334 sets de cocina. A la fecha, se han entregado 830 lonas y 825 sets de cocina.

En el Polideportivo José María Vargas, un equipo de 12 profesionales brindó 290 consultas de salud y salud mental, incluidas atenciones de odontología, medicina interna, pediatría, ginecología y obstetricia, psiquiatría y psicología.

El registro continuó en el Estadio César Nieves, con 489 familias y 1.479 personas, y en Playa Grande, con 54 familias y 160 personas. También se avanzó en la Evaluación Rápida de Necesidades y en el apoyo de gestión de información al Grupo de Trabajo de Infraestructura y Alojamientos Temporales.

Respuesta planificada de la OIM

La OIM continuará enfocándose en la adecuación y gestión de campamentos transitorios, WASH, salud y SMAPS, protección, registro, evaluaciones de necesidades, seguimiento de la movilidad y coordinación interagencial.

El financiamiento adicional permitiría ampliar la asistencia de alojamiento, artículos esenciales, recuperación temprana, protección, salud y salud mental, datos, DTM y apoyo a la coordinación.

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Venezuela: Latinoamérica y El Caribe Resumen de Situación Semanal al 10 de julio 2026
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Countries: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of), Colombia, Haiti
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached Infographic.

CIFRAS CLAVE

  • 3,9K personas fallecidas tras dos poderosos terremotos que sacudieron Venezuela el 24 de junio
  • 7,1K personas desplazadas debido a la violencia en el departamento de Ouest de Haití desde el 1 de julio
  • 4M de personas podrían necesitar asistencia alimentaria entre junio y enero en Colombia

VENEZUELA: TERREMOTO

Más de dos semanas después de los terremotos de magnitud 7,2 y 7,5 del 24 de junio en la región centro-norte de Venezuela, las autoridades informan de 3.889 personas fallecidas, 16.740 heridas y 6.462 rescatadas, con más de 1.100 réplicas registradas. Al 9 de julio, 87 campamentos transitorios siguen brindando apoyo a 16.891 personas en Caracas, La Guaira y Miranda. Los equipos internacionales de búsqueda y rescate urbano han comenzado a desmovilizarse mientras las autoridades nacionales de Protección Civil asumen las operaciones restantes. Tras visitar las comunidades afectadas y reunirse con socios humanitarios nacionales e internacionales, el Subsecretario General de las Naciones Unidas para Asuntos Humanitarios y Coordinador del Socorro de Emergencia, Tom Fletcher, anunció un llamamiento por 298 millones de dólares adicionales para cubrir las necesidades de 1,3 millones de personas en los próximos seis meses.

Las necesidades prioritarias siguen concentradas en alojamiento, salud, agua, saneamiento e higiene (WASH), protección, salud mental y apoyo psicosocial. Save the Children ha expresado preocupación por el bienestar de los aproximadamente 680.000 niñas y niños afectados, cuyos padres y psicólogos informan de signos de angustia aguda. El último análisis del Clúster de Protección, liderado por ACNUR, también señala un aumento de los riesgos de separación familiar, violencia de género y exclusión de la asistencia entre menores, mujeres, personas mayores y personas con discapacidad, impulsados por alojamientos superpoblados, redes de apoyo familiares y comunitarias interrumpidas, y brechas en los servicios para quienes se alojan informalmente fuera de los campamentos designados. A medida que la fase de emergencia va llegando a su fin, los actores humanitarios están priorizando la ampliación del acceso y fortalecer la capacidad para identificar y responder a estos riesgos en el futuro.

Apoya aquí al Fondo Humanitario Venezolano

Para más información, visita: ReliefWeb Response

HAITÍ: VIOLENCIA Y DESPLAZAMIENTO

Dos oleadas separadas de violencia armada en el departamento Ouest de Haití desplazaron a unas 7.168 personas durante la primera semana de julio. Los enfrentamientos del 1 de julio en las localidades de Pinso y Grand-État, en la comuna de Ganthier, desplazaron a 1.328 personas (262 hogares), todas las cuales se refugiaron con familias de acogida dentro del municipio. En Kenscoff, los ataques que comenzaron la noche del 4 al 5 de julio y se extendieron el 8 de julio desplazaron a 5.840 personas (1.836 hogares). La violencia, que resultó en pérdida de vidas, incendios de viviendas y secuestros, también obligó al cierre de tres sitios preexistentes, desplazando a personas que ya se encontraban desplazadas. La mayoría se aloja con familias de acogida, mientras que el 14% se ha trasladado a dos sitios. El incidente supone el mayor desplazamiento de la zona desde febrero de 2025.

COLOMBIA: SEGURIDAD ALIMENTARIA

Una combinación de choques meteorológicos, inseguridad localizada y presiones sobre los precios están impulsando la inseguridad alimentaria en algunas partes de Colombia. Según el último informe de FEWS NET, se prevé que los niveles de Crisis (Fase 3 de la CIF) de inseguridad alimentaria persistan hasta enero de 2027 en los departamentos de La Guajira y Chocó. En La Guajira, las condiciones de El Niño que se están desarrollando agravan los impactos de la sequía en el ganado y los cultivos, mientras que, en Chocó, el riesgo persistente de inundaciones y la inseguridad continúan interrumpiendo el transporte fluvial y el acceso a los mercados, especialmente en la subregión de Baudó. Se prevé que la población que necesita asistencia humanitaria alimentaria en el país aumente gradualmente hasta situarse entre 4,0 y 4,99 millones de personas, identificándose a los hogares rurales pobres, indígenas, afrocolombianos, migrantes venezolanos y de la economía informal urbana como las poblaciones que suscitan mayor preocupación.

Los equipos internacionales de búsqueda y rescate urbano han comenzado a desmovilizarse mientras las autoridades nacionales de Protección Civil asumen las operaciones restantes. Tras visitar las comunidades afectadas y reunirse con socios humanitarios nacionales e internacionales, el Subsecretario General de las Naciones Unidas para Asuntos Humanitarios y Coordinador del Socorro de Emergencia, Tom Fletcher, anunció un llamamiento por 298 millones de dólares adicionales para cubrir las necesidades de 1,3 millones de personas en los próximos seis meses.
Las necesidades prioritarias siguen concentradas en alojamiento, salud, agua, saneamiento e higiene (WASH), protección, salud mental y apoyo psicosocial. Save the Children ha expresado preocupación por el bienestar de los aproximadamente 680.000 niñas y niños afectados, cuyos padres y psicólogos informan de signos de angustia aguda. El último análisis del Clúster de Protección, liderado por ACNUR, también señala un aumento de los riesgos de separación familiar, violencia de género y exclusión de la asistencia entre menores, mujeres, personas mayores y personas con discapacidad, impulsados por alojamientos superpoblados, redes de apoyo familiares y comunitarias interrumpidas, y brechas en los servicios para quienes se alojan informalmente fuera de los campamentos designados. A medida que la fase de emergencia va llegando a su fin, los actores humanitarios están priorizando la ampliación del acceso y fortalecer la capacidad para identificar y responder a estos riesgos en el futuro.
Apoya aquí al Fondo Humanitario Venezolano
Para más información, visita: ReliefWeb Response
HAITÍ: VIOLENCIA Y DESPLAZAMIENTO
Dos oleadas separadas de violencia armada en el departamento Ouest de Haití desplazaron a unas 7.168 personas durante la primera semana de julio. Los enfrentamientos del 1 de julio en las localidades de Pinso y Grand-État, en la comuna de Ganthier, desplazaron a 1.328 personas (262 hogares), todas las cuales se refugiaron con familias de acogida dentro del municipio. En Kenscoff, los ataques que comenzaron la noche del 4 al 5 de julio y se extendieron el 8 de julio desplazaron a 5.840 personas (1.836 hogares). La violencia, que resultó en pérdida de vidas, incendios de viviendas y secuestros, también obligó al cierre de tres sitios preexistentes, desplazando a personas que ya se encontraban desplazadas. La mayoría se aloja con familias de acogida, mientras que el 14% se ha trasladado a dos sitios. El incidente supone el mayor desplazamiento de la zona desde febrero de 2025.
COLOMBIA: SEGURIDAD ALIMENTARIA
Una combinación de choques meteorológicos, inseguridad localizada y presiones sobre los precios están impulsando la inseguridad alimentaria en algunas partes de Colombia. Según el último informe de FEWS NET, se prevé que los niveles de Crisis (Fase 3 de la CIF) de inseguridad alimentaria persistan hasta enero de 2027 en los departamentos de La Guajira y Chocó. En La Guajira, las condiciones de El Niño que se están desarrollando agravan los impactos de la sequía en el ganado y los cultivos, mientras que, en Chocó, el riesgo persistente de inundaciones y la inseguridad continúan interrumpiendo el transporte fluvial y el acceso a los mercados, especialmente en la subregión de Baudó. Se prevé que la población que necesita asistencia humanitaria alimentaria en el país aumente gradualmente hasta situarse entre 4,0 y 4,99 millones de personas, identificándose a los hogares rurales pobres, indígenas, afrocolombianos, migrantes venezolanos y de la economía informal urbana como las poblaciones que suscitan mayor preocupación.
categories: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Venezuela: Latin America & The Caribbean Weekly Situation Update as of 10 July 2026
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Countries: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of), Colombia, Haiti
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached Infographic.

KEY FIGURES

  • 3.9K people dead after two powerful earthquakes struck Venezuela on 24 June
  • 7.1K people displaced due to violence in Haiti’s Ouest department since 1 July
  • 4M people may require food assistance between June-January in Colombia

VENEZUELA: EARTHQUAKE

More than two weeks after the 24 June magnitude 7.2 and 7.5 earthquakes in Venezuela’s north-central region, authorities report 3,889 people dead, 16,740 injured, and 6,462 rescued, with more than 1,100 aftershocks recorded. As of 9 July, 87 transitional camps remain supporting 16,891 people across Caracas, La Guaira and Miranda. International Urban Search and Rescue teams have begun demobilizing as national Civil Protection authorities take over remaining operations. Following visits to affected communities and meetings with national and international humanitarian partners, UN Under-Secretary-General for Humanitarian Affairs and Emergency Relief Coordinator Tom Fletcher announced an appeal for an additional US$298 million to meet the needs of 1.3 million people over the next six months.

Priority needs remain concentrated in shelter, health, water, sanitation and hygiene (WASH), protection, mental health and psychosocial support. Save the Children has raised concerns about the wellbeing of the roughly 680,000 affected children, with parents and psychologists reporting signs of acute distress. The UNHCR-led Protection Cluster’s latest analysis also points to heightened risks of family separation, gender-based violence, and exclusion from assistance among children, women, older persons, and people with disabilities, driven by overcrowded shelters, disrupted family and community support networks, and service gaps for those sheltering informally outside designated camps. As the emergency phase winds down, humanitarian actors are prioritizing expanded access and stronger capacity to identify and respond to these risks going forward.

Support the Venezuelan Humanitarian Fund here

For more information, visit: ReliefWeb Response

HAITI: VIOLENCE & DISPLACEMENT

Two separate waves of armed violence in Haiti’s Ouest Department displaced an estimated 7,168 people over the first week of July. Clashes on 1 July in the localities of Pinso and Grand-État, Ganthier commune, displaced 1,328 people (262 households), all of whom took refuge with host families within the municipality. In Kenscoff, attacks beginning the night of 4-5 July and spreading on 8 July displaced 5,840 people (1,836 households). The violence, which resulted in loss of life, house burnings and kidnappings, also forced three pre-existing sites to close, displacing people previously displaced. The majority are sheltering with host families while 14 per cent moved into two sites. The incident marks the area’s largest displacement event since February 2025.

COLOMBIA: FOOD SECURITY

A combination of weather shocks, localized insecurity and price pressures are driving food insecurity in parts of Colombia. According to FEWS NET’s latest outlook, crisis (IPC Phase 3) levels of food insecurity are expected to persist through January 2027 in the departments of La Guajira and Chocó. In La Guajira, developing El Niño conditions are worsening drought impacts on livestock and crops, while in Chocó, persistent flood risk and insecurity continue to disrupt river transport and market access, particularly in the Baudó subregion. The population requiring humanitarian food assistance across the country is expected to rise gradually to between 4.0 and 4.99 million people, with poor rural, Indigenous, Afro-Colombian, Venezuelan migrant, and informal urban households identified as populations of highest concern.

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Haiti: Bulletin humanitaire OCHA (Mars–Mai 2026)
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Haiti
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

  • Insécurité alimentaire : près de 5,8 millions de personnes (52 % de la population) sont en insécurité alimentaire aiguë, dont environ 1,9 million en phase d’urgence.
  • Déplacements massifs liés à la violence : plus de 1,5 million de personnes déplacées internes, aggravant les vulnérabilités et la pression sur les ressources.
  • Crise agricole en Artibonite : les violences perturbent fortement la production, avec des agriculteurs contraints d’abandonner leurs terres et 5 à 8 % des ménages agricoles directement affectés dans les zones les plus touchées.
  • Violences basées sur le genre en forte hausse : 1 927 incidents rapportés entre janvier et mars 2026, dont plus de 70 % de viols, majoritairement liés à des groupes armés, et près de 70 % des survivantes de VBG sont des personnes déplacées, avec un accès très limité aux services de prise en charge.
  • Inclusion humanitaire insuffisante : 80 % des femmes et filles handicapées vivent dans des zones affectées par la violence, tandis que 82 % des organisations manquent de capacités pour répondre à leurs besoins.

Country: Haiti

Violences basées sur le genre en forte hausse : 1 927 incidents rapportés entre janvier et mars 2026, dont plus de 70 % de viols, majoritairement liés à des groupes armés, et près de 70 % des survivantes de VBG sont des personnes déplacées, avec un accès très limité aux services de prise en charge.
Inclusion humanitaire insuffisante : 80 % des femmes et filles handicapées vivent dans des zones affectées par la violence, tandis que 82 % des organisations manquent de capacités pour répondre à leurs besoins.

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Venezuela Earthquakes: Private Sector Contributions Snapshot (As of 9 July 2026)
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached Infographic.

This snapshot provides an overview of the private sector humanitarian response to the June 2026 earthquakes in Venezuela.

As of 9 July 2026, businesses and private foundations have mobilized *US$ 32.9 million* in support of affected communities and humanitarian partners. This response, tracked by the OCHA-UNDP Connecting Business Initiative (CBi), includes both cash contributions and in-kind support, such as critical relief supplies and heavy machinery for search and rescue operations.

Key Highlights

  • Total Resources Mobilized: US$ 32.9 million
  • Private Sector Partners: 27
  • Recipient Organizations: 14

This document outlines the contribution modalities, highlights featured private sector commitments, and lists the partners currently supporting the humanitarian efforts

items.creator: UN Women
items.title: Venezuela Earthquakes — UN Women Humanitarian Update
items.author: UN Women
items.content:
Country: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Source: UN Women

Please refer to the attached file.

This humanitarian update examines the differentiated impact of the earthquakes in Venezuela on women and girls and identifies the key priorities for integrating the gender dimension into the humanitarian response. The report highlights risks such as gender-based violence, the increased burden of unpaid care work, barriers to accessing health services, and the impacts on mental health and livelihoods.

It also outlines the actions led by UN Women in collaboration with women-led organizations, the United Nations system, and other partners, and calls for strengthened funding and coordination to support an inclusive recovery that addresses the specific needs of women and girls.

To learn more about UN Women's response, please visit Venezuela Earthquakes — Humanitarian Update | Publications | UN Women – Americas and the Caribbean

items.creator: El Equipo Humanitario País Colombia
items.title: Colombia: Instructivo para Reporte en el sistema 5W –Planes de respuesta HNRP y RMRP–
items.author: El Equipo Humanitario País Colombia
items.content:
Country: Colombia
Sources: El Equipo Humanitario País Colombia, Grupo Interagencial sobre Flujos Migratorios Mixtos, UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

La finalidad de este documento es fortalecer la eficiencia y coherencia en el reporte de las actividades de respuesta humanitaria, teniendo en cuenta el primer ciclo de reporte que inició a partir de marzo de 2026, dirigido a todas las organizaciones socias del EHP, GIFMM y/o del GTM Nacional, quienes comenzaron a reportar su información en una única plataforma: ActivityInfo (5W). Esta herramienta permite reportar las actividades de respuesta correspondientes a los Planes de Respuesta vigentes (HNRP y RMRP) evitando el uso de múltiples sistemas y reduciendo duplicidades durante el reporte. Este instructivo ha sido elaborado para facilitar ese proceso, ofrecer claridad sobre los pasos a seguir y garantizar que la información reportada sea precisa, consistente y alineada con los requerimientos establecidos.

items.creator: Insecurity Insight
items.title: Cameroon: Violence Against Health Care in Conflict 2025
items.author: Insecurity Insight
items.content:
Country: Cameroon
Sources: Insecurity Insight, Safeguarding Health in Conflict

Please refer to the attached file.

Repeated Violence and Insecurity Continued to Affect the Functioning of Health Services in Cameroon in 2025

Safeguarding Health in Conflict Coalition (SHCC)

Using data collected by Insecurity Insight, the Safeguarding Health in Conflict Coalition (SHCC) identified 30 incidentsof violence against or obstruction of health care in Cameroon in 2025. 18 health workers were kidnapped and nine arrested.

The report examines patterns of attacks affecting health workers, facilities, patients, and transport amid intensifying conflict between armed groups, and explores the consequences for access to health care, particularly in remote and conflict-affected regions. The report notes that the true scale of attacks is likely higher than documented due to underreporting in inaccessible areas and reduced humanitarian presence, including the impact of cuts to USAID funding on health service provision and monitoring.

The report forms part of SHCC's annual report'Care in the Crosshairs'.

Key findings:

  • For the second consecutive year, reported health worker kidnappings in Northwest region increased, with Ambazonian separatists and Bui Unity Warriors often named as perpetrators.
  • Gendarmerie forces in Northwest and Southwest regions continued to arrest health workers, accusing them of links to opposition groups. Health workers were mostly harmed while on their way to or from work, at home, or pursuing a private activity, highlighting how their vulnerability is heightened in their non-working lives within the community.
  • Lockdowns enforced by non-state armed groups and movement restrictions linked to armed clashes limited health workers’ and patients’ ability to travel to obtain health care and disrupted routine preventive health care activities. Insecurity, movement restrictions, and access constraints during lockdown periods forced humanitarian partners to suspend or adapt field operations, resulting in operational delays and reduced capacity to implement activities in certain areas and constraining the delivery of medical assistance.

Full report available in English and French.

Insecurity Insight updates data continuously and numbers may change if new information is made publicly available. For the latest available figures, see Insecurity Insight’s global map on attacks on health care since 2016.

Other resources

Drawing on a decade of systematically collected data, Still Under Attackdocuments the scale, patterns, and consequences of attacks on health care in conflict-affected settings worldwide since the adoption of UN Security Council Resolution 2286 in 2016:

Based exclusively on Insecurity Insight’s data collection, it highlights how violence against health care is becoming normalised, with serious consequences for civilians, disease response and health systems.

The report provides detailed analysis of perpetrators, methods of attack and emerging risks, including the growing role of new technologies in conflict environments. It also offers recommendations for governments, international organisations, humanitarian actors and the diplomatic community aimed at strengthening the protection of health care and improving accountability for violations.

Published every two weeks, the Attacks on Health News Brief comprises threats and violence as well as protests and other events affecting the delivery of and access to health care around the world.

items.creator: Radio Ergo
items.title: Somalia: Radio Ergo Weekly Feedback Report, Issued: 10 July 2026
items.author: Radio Ergo
items.content:
Country: Somalia
Source: Radio Ergo

Please refer to the attached file.

Brief Summary

The calls to Radio Ergo’s independent nationwide audience feedback platform in the week 2-9 July 2026 were dominated in terms of numbers by people from Hirshabelle, South West, and Jubbaland states citing good rainfall and livelihood recovery. The next most popular topic was livestock disease, with callers from the same states as well as a few from central and further north describing conditions affecting goats and camels. In addition, callers mainly from parts of Puntland and central locations complained of continued or a return to dire drought conditions. On the other hand, some farmers in parts of Hirshabelle spoke of healthy crops and even bumper harvests. The first reports on the Ergo platform this year of locust invasions came from Galgadud and Hiran. The following summarises the calls by theme.

Food security – several farmers in Hiran and Middle and Lower Shabelle regions said they had benefited from good rainfall and that their crops and livestock were doing well. One caller in Qoryolley described their harvests as bumper. On the other hand, a caller in Guriel said jobs in manual labour such as stone breaking were scarce. Another in Kismayo said small businesses were suffering from a low ebb in trade.

Floods – one caller in Hanoley, Middle Shabelle, said they were concerned about river floods that had inundated farms. He said they had to cross flood water with their livestock as they took them out to find grazing.

Conflict & Protection – a caller in western Hiran region said that Al-Shabab militia had killed many people and burned properties in the area. He called on the government to help the community.

Locusts - a caller in Hiran said their crops had failed due to a locust invasion and water shortages. Another in Adado, Galgadud, said they were concerned by a locust invasion.

Health – a caller in Hargeisa noted mosquito infestations with a lack of access to bed nets, whilst another in an unspecified location said that chikungunya disease was spreading rampantly in their community.

Drought & water shortage – a caller in Bali-Nasiye, Togdher region, said the Gu seasonal rainfall had been poor and they were now heading to the dry season. He appealed for aid. A caller in Gumbah, Bari region, said they were facing prolonged drought with acute water scarcity. He added that the water wells had dried up and they had not seen aid organizations recently. A number of people called from parts of Galgadud including Adado, Abudwak, Dusamareb, Guriel and Ramale. A female caller in Abudwak said their goats were dying. A caller in Adado said many families had migrated in search of water and fodder and those without means were left behind. Among those in Guriel a female caller said the rains had come and then ceased and they were now in the Hagaa windy season, and the livestock had no water or fodder. A caller in Ramale said their livestock continued to die off.

Livestock – callers from various regions cited goat diseases including measles, CCPP, and cowpox. Many described symptoms such as diarrhoea, mouth sores, coughing, body swelling, weakness, deaths, and miscarriages. In Baidoa, a caller said their camels were sick with swollen bodies. A caller in Qamqam, Lower Juba, said a pregnant camel had developed fever overnight and they had slaughtered it before it died. He wanted veterinary advice. Another said their camels had bloody looking diarrhoea.

Rainfall – several callers in parts of Galgadud and Hiran cited beneficial rainfall. In Middle Shabelle, people in various locations said livestock were doing well and water sources were replenished. One in Halgan said the river level was rising. Callers in Bay, Gedo, Middle and Lower Juba cited good rainfall and livelihood improvements.

Feedback – a notable number of callers this week commended Radio Ergo for its awareness programmes and news and information services.
items.contentSnippet:
Country: Somalia
Source: Radio Ergo

Please refer to the attached file.
Brief Summary
The calls to Radio Ergo’s independent nationwide audience feedback platform in the week 2-9 July 2026 were dominated in terms of numbers by people from Hirshabelle, South West, and Jubbaland states citing good rainfall and livelihood recovery. The next most popular topic was livestock disease, with callers from the same states as well as a few from central and further north describing conditions affecting goats and camels. In addition, callers mainly from parts of Puntland and central locations complained of continued or a return to dire drought conditions. On the other hand, some farmers in parts of Hirshabelle spoke of healthy crops and even bumper harvests. The first reports on the Ergo platform this year of locust invasions came from Galgadud and Hiran. The following summarises the calls by theme.
Food security – several farmers in Hiran and Middle and Lower Shabelle regions said they had benefited from good rainfall and that their crops and livestock were doing well. One caller in Qoryolley described their harvests as bumper. On the other hand, a caller in Guriel said jobs in manual labour such as stone breaking were scarce. Another in Kismayo said small businesses were suffering from a low ebb in trade.
Floods – one caller in Hanoley, Middle Shabelle, said they were concerned about river floods that had inundated farms. He said they had to cross flood water with their livestock as they took them out to find grazing.
Conflict & Protection – a caller in western Hiran region said that Al-Shabab militia had killed many people and burned properties in the area. He called on the government to help the community.
Locusts - a caller in Hiran said their crops had failed due to a locust invasion and water shortages. Another in Adado, Galgadud, said they were concerned by a locust invasion.
Health – a caller in Hargeisa noted mosquito infestations with a lack of access to bed nets, whilst another in an unspecified location said that chikungunya disease was spreading rampantly in their community.
Drought & water shortage – a caller in Bali-Nasiye, Togdher region, said the Gu seasonal rainfall had been poor and they were now heading to the dry season. He appealed for aid. A caller in Gumbah, Bari region, said they were facing prolonged drought with acute water scarcity. He added that the water wells had dried up and they had not seen aid organizations recently. A number of people called from parts of Galgadud including Adado, Abudwak, Dusamareb, Guriel and Ramale. A female caller in Abudwak said their goats were dying. A caller in Adado said many families had migrated in search of water and fodder and those without means were left behind. Among those in Guriel a female caller said the rains had come and then ceased and they were now in the Hagaa windy season, and the livestock had no water or fodder. A caller in Ramale said their livestock continued to die off.
Livestock – callers from various regions cited goat diseases including measles, CCPP, and cowpox. Many described symptoms such as diarrhoea, mouth sores, coughing, body swelling, weakness, deaths, and miscarriages. In Baidoa, a caller said their camels were sick with swollen bodies. A caller in Qamqam, Lower Juba, said a pregnant camel had developed fever overnight and they had slaughtered it before it died. He wanted veterinary advice. Another said their camels had bloody looking diarrhoea.
Rainfall – several callers in parts of Galgadud and Hiran cited beneficial rainfall. In Middle Shabelle, people in various locations said livestock were doing well and water sources were replenished. One in Halgan said the river level was rising. Callers in Bay, Gedo, Middle and Lower Juba cited good rainfall and livelihood improvements.
Feedback – a notable number of callers this week commended Radio Ergo for its awareness programmes and news and information services.
